does: This paper surveys the current landscape of risks inherent in large language models and evaluates existing guardrail and alignment approaches designed to mitigate them. It systematically documents hazards including bias, unsafe outputs, dataset poisoning, hallucinations, and lack of explainability that emerge from LLM deployment in sensitive applications. The work examines both intrinsic evaluation methods (measuring model properties directly) and extrinsic approaches (assessing real-world behavior), presents fairness metrics for responsible development, and analyzes technical strategies for securing LLMs through a layered protection framework operating at external, secondary, and internal levels. It addresses the particular challenges of agentic LLMs capable of taking real-world actions, emphasizing requirements for testability, fail-safes, and situational awareness. The paper synthesizes current guardrail techniques including system prompts and other alignment mechanisms to provide a comprehensive assessment of how well existing defenses address documented risks.
connects: This work builds on foundational research in AI safety and alignment that has grown substantially as LLMs became more capable and widely deployed. It extends earlier work on bias detection and fairness in machine learning systems by applying these concepts specifically to large language models, connecting to streams of research on dataset poisoning, adversarial robustness, and model interpretability. The paper engages with the emerging literature on LLM alignment techniques such as reinforcement learning from human feedback and constitutional AI, building on philosophical and technical work addressing how to ensure AI systems behave according to human values. It also relates to work on agentic AI systems and their safety implications, connecting concerns about autonomous agents taking actions in the real world to the specific challenges posed by LLMs with tool use and planning capabilities.
enables: This comprehensive risk assessment and guardrail evaluation provides a foundation for developing more robust safety frameworks as LLMs continue to scale and proliferate. By systematically cataloging risks and current mitigation approaches, the work enables researchers to identify gaps where existing defenses remain insufficient, pointing toward needed research in areas like interpretability, robustness, and reliable containment of agentic systems. The layered protection model offers a conceptual framework that practitioners and developers can use to design defense-in-depth strategies tailored to specific applications. The emphasis on testability and situational awareness for autonomous LLM systems establishes benchmarks for evaluating future alignment approaches. Additionally, the synthesis of fairness and bias evaluation methods enables more standardized practices for responsible LLM deployment, facilitating better cross-institutional comparison of safety properties and supporting regulatory frameworks for high-stakes applications.
